questions.question: Explain why sound travels faster in solids than in air.
questions.answer: Particles in solids are closer, so vibrations transmit faster, making sound travel faster in solids.

questions.question: Why does sound need a medium to propagate?
questions.answer: Sound is a mechanical wave; it requires particles of a medium to vibrate and transmit energy.

questions.question: Define reflection of sound.
questions.answer: Reflection of sound is the bouncing back of sound waves from a surface without loss of energy.

questions.question: Why do we hear an echo clearly only if the reflecting surface is at least 17 m away?
questions.answer: Sound travels 343 m/s; for a delay of 0.1 s (minimum for human ear to distinguish), distance should be ≥ 17 m.

questions.question: What is the relation between wavelength, frequency, and speed of sound?
questions.answer: Speed of sound (v) = Frequency (f) × Wavelength (λ).

questions.question: How does temperature affect the speed of sound in air?
questions.answer: Higher air temperature increases particle vibration speed, thus increasing the speed of sound.

questions.question: Why does sound become fainter as we move away from the source?
questions.answer: Energy spreads over larger area; intensity decreases, so sound becomes fainter.

questions.question: What is the difference between audible sound, ultrasound, and infrasound?
questions.answer: Audible: 20 Hz–20,000 Hz, Ultrasound: >20,000 Hz, Infrasound: <20 Hz.

questions.question: Explain why a musical instrument produces a sound of definite pitch.
questions.answer: Definite frequency of vibration of the instrument produces sound of definite pitch.

questions.question: Define intensity of sound.
questions.answer: Intensity is the sound energy transmitted per unit area per second perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

questions.question: How does amplitude of vibration affect loudness?
questions.answer: Greater amplitude → more energy → louder sound.

questions.question: Why are echoes not heard clearly in a small room?
questions.answer: Reflections reach the ear too quickly; the delay is less than 0.1 s, so echo is not distinguishable.

questions.question: Explain reverberation in auditoriums.
questions.answer: Multiple reflections of sound cause it to persist after source stops; design uses sound-absorbing materials to reduce it.

questions.question: Why do bats use ultrasound?
questions.answer: Bats use ultrasound for echolocation to navigate and locate prey in the dark.

questions.question: What is the function of the human ear in hearing sound?
questions.answer: The ear detects vibrations in the air, converts them into electrical signals, and sends them to the brain.

questions.question: Explain why sound cannot travel in vacuum.
questions.answer: Vacuum has no particles to transmit vibrations, so mechanical waves like sound cannot propagate.

questions.question: Why do metals sound louder than wood when struck?
questions.answer: Metals are denser and transmit vibrations faster with less energy loss, producing louder sound.

questions.question: What is the audible range of the human ear?
questions.answer: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz.

questions.question: Why does a guitar string produce different notes when plucked at different lengths?
questions.answer: Effective length changes frequency of vibration; shorter length → higher frequency → higher pitch.

questions.question: How do noise-cancelling headphones reduce unwanted sound?
questions.answer: They produce sound waves of same amplitude but opposite phase to cancel external noise.

questions.question: Define wavelength of sound wave.
questions.answer: Distance between two consecutive compressions or rarefactions in a sound wave.

questions.question: What is the relationship between pitch and frequency?
questions.answer: Higher frequency → higher pitch, Lower frequency → lower pitch.

questions.question: Why do sound waves bend around obstacles?
questions.answer: Diffraction allows sound waves to bend around obstacles or pass through openings.

questions.question: Give one application of ultrasound in industry.
questions.answer: Ultrasound is used to detect cracks in metals (non-destructive testing).

questions.question: Why does the sound of a bell become fainter with distance?
questions.answer: Sound energy spreads over a larger area and some energy is absorbed by air, decreasing intensity.
